summary: Jai anand worked on map distance problems with decimals, and overall he showed a solid understanding of how to trace routes and combine distances. His strongest work was on direct path totals, finding missing distances when a whole route was given, and comparing two travel distances to see how much farther one destination was than another. The harder area was multi-step comparison problems and some shortest-route questions where he had to first build each full path and then compare or choose between routes. In those problems, he often seemed to use only part of the route or compare the wrong segments. The next step is to slow him down and give him a simple routine: first find the full distance to each destination, then compare, subtract, or choose the shorter total. With that structure, he should be able to turn this into a strong skill.

correct.details: These problems ask for the total distance between two places by following one connected route and adding all labeled segments on that route. Jai anand showed he can read the map path and combine decimal distances accurately.

correct.category: Finding a missing segment from a total route
correct.details: These problems give the total distance for a route and one known part, so the missing distance must be found by subtraction. Jai anand handled these part-whole decimal distance problems correctly.
These questions ask how much farther one destination is than another from the same starting point. Jai anand successfully found each full route distance and then compared the totals.

These problems require testing more than one possible path and selecting the route with the smaller total distance. Jai anand did well adding decimal segments and checking which complete route was shortest.

incorrect.details: These problems ask for one total route distance by adding connected decimal segments between the start and end locations.
incorrect.whyWrong: Jai anand likely added the wrong pair of segments, missed one segment, or treated a nearby label as the answer instead of building the full route from start to finish.
incorrect.howToTeach: Teach him to trace the route with a finger or pencil from the starting town to the ending town and say each segment aloud before adding. A helpful sentence frame is: first path, then numbers, then total.
incorrect.ifTheyUnderstand: If he understands, he will consistently add all segments on the actual route and ignore extra labels that are not part of the trip.

incorrect.category: Finding a missing segment from a total route
incorrect.details: These are part-whole map problems where the total route is known and one unknown segment must be found by subtracting the known pieces from the whole.
incorrect.whyWrong: He appears to have treated the problem like addition, or subtracted the wrong amount because he did not first identify which segments make up the given total route.
incorrect.howToTeach: Have him mark the full route that matches the stated total, add the known parts on that route, and then subtract from the total. Emphasize the question: what piece is missing from the whole trip?
incorrect.ifTheyUnderstand: If he understands, he will be able to explain which distances are part of the total route and use subtraction only after identifying the whole and the known parts.

incorrect.details: These questions ask how much farther one destination is than another, so each destination's full route must be found before comparing the totals.
incorrect.whyWrong: Jai anand often seems to have compared single segments instead of full route totals, or subtracted too early before finishing both paths. On some items, very close decimal values may also have made the final subtraction harder.
incorrect.howToTeach: Use a two-column routine: route A total, route B total, then difference. Do not subtract until both totals are complete. Encourage him to write each path as a number sentence before comparing.
incorrect.ifTheyUnderstand: If he understands, he will first compute both complete distances and then find how much farther by subtracting the smaller total from the larger total.

incorrect.details: This type of problem requires checking more than one possible path and choosing the complete route with the smallest total distance.
incorrect.whyWrong: He likely found one possible route and stopped, instead of comparing all reasonable routes to see which total was actually shortest.
incorrect.howToTeach: Teach him to list each possible route, add each total, and then circle the smallest. Hearing the phrase shortest means compare all routes can help build the habit.
incorrect.ifTheyUnderstand: If he understands, he will test multiple full routes and justify why the chosen one has the smallest total.

glossary.explanation: A route is the path you travel from one place to another on the map.
Distance tells how far it is between places.
Total distance is the full length of a trip after adding all parts of the route.
Missing distance is the unknown part of a route that can be found from the whole and the known parts.
The shortest route is the path with the smallest total distance.
This means finding the difference between two total distances.
A segment is one labeled part of a route between two places.
Subtract means take one amount away from another to find the difference or a missing part.
A decimal is a number with a point, such as 7.5, used to show parts of a whole unit.
